comment: Optional per-word memory hooks, keyed concept -> target language -> support language. Missing entries are fine — the UI only shows a note when one exists. Authored with AI assistance.

pt.en: grande — same root as English 'grand' (and the coffee size).
bom — from Latin bonus ('good'); a bonbon is literally a 'good-good' treat.
ruim — think 'ruined': something ruined has gone bad.
novo — same root as 'novel' and 'innovation'.
velho — from Latin vetulus; think 'veteran' (an old hand).
fácil — same root as English 'facile' (easy).
difícil — near-twin of English 'difficult'.
dele — literally de + ele, 'of him'.
dela — literally de + ela, 'of her'.
nosso — from Latin noster, as in 'Cosa Nostra' ('our thing').
deles — literally de + eles, 'of them'.
branco — same root as 'blank' and French 'blanc'.
vermelho — same root as 'vermilion', the bright red pigment.
azul — same root as 'azure'.
verde — same root as 'verdant' (lush green).
amarelo — twin of Spanish 'amarillo', as in Amarillo, Texas.
laranja — cousin of 'orange': both trace back to Arabic naranj.
rápido — same root as 'rapid'.
lento — the musical tempo marking 'lento' means slow.
longo — near-twin of English 'long'.
curto — same root as 'curt' (short and brusque).
pesado — from peso ('weight'), like the currency; heavy = weighty.
leve — same root as 'levity' and 'levitate' (lightness).
escuro — from Latin obscurus; think 'obscure' (dark, hidden).
jovem — same root as 'juvenile'.
direito — same root as 'direct'; covers the right side and legal rights.
esquerdo — sounds a bit like 'skewed': veering off to the left.
correto — near-twin of English 'correct'.
errado — same root as 'error': full of error = wrong.
legal — literally 'legal', but in Brazil it's everyday slang for 'cool, nice'.
frio — same root as 'frigid'.
mas — careful: one letter away from mais ('more'); mas = but.
também — literally tão + bem, 'as well'.
porque — literally por + que, 'for the reason that'.
topo — near-twin of English 'top'.
baixo — same root as 'bass' (the low voice) and 'base'.
embaixo — em + baixo, 'in the low part'; baixo is akin to 'bass' (low).
ao lado — literally 'at the side'; lado is akin to 'lateral'.
frente — same root as 'front' (Latin frons).
fora — from Latin foras ('outside'), the root of 'foreign'.
entre — Latin inter, as in 'international' (between nations).
volta — a turn or return; think 'revolve/revolution'.
fora — from Latin foras ('outside'), the root of 'foreign'.
norte — near-twin of English 'north'.
sul — borrowed from the same Germanic word as English 'south'.
leste — via French l'est, from the same Germanic word as 'east'.
oeste — via French ouest, from the same Germanic word as 'west'.
com — the Latin com- in 'companion' and 'combine': together with.
água — same root as 'aqua' and 'aquarium'.
comida — from comer ('to eat'); think 'comestibles'.
casa — as in Casablanca ('white house') or 'mi casa es su casa'.
lar — from the Lares, the Roman household gods who guarded the home.
livro — from Latin liber; same root as 'library'.
coisa — from Latin causa, the same origin as English 'cause'.
trabalho — same root as English 'travail' (hard work).
quarto — think 'living quarters'; it also means 'quarter/fourth'.
menino — starts like 'mini': a mini human, a little boy.
menina — menino's feminine: think 'mini' plus the feminine -a.
homem — from Latin homo, as in 'Homo sapiens'.
pai — sounds like 'pie'; same 'papa' family as English 'pa'.
filho — same root as 'filial' (of a son or daughter).
filha — feminine of filho; same root as 'filial'.
irmão — from Latin germanus ('full sibling'); think 'germane' (closely related).
irmã — irmão's feminine; same Latin germanus ('sibling') root.
cabeça — from Latin caput, the root of 'capital' and 'captain'.
braço — same root as 'bracelet' (worn on the arm) and 'embrace'.
mão — from Latin manus, as in 'manual' and 'manicure'.
pé — from Latin pes; same root as 'pedal' and 'pedestrian'.
dedo — from Latin digitus; think 'digit'.
olho — from Latin oculus; think 'ocular' and 'binoculars'.
boca — as in Boca Raton; think 'buccal' (of the mouth).
café da manhã — literally 'coffee of the morning'.
roupas — same Germanic root as 'robe'.
camisa — same root as 'camisole'.
telefone — near-twin of 'telephone'.
dois — from Latin duo; think 'duo' or 'duet'.
três — think 'trio' and 'triple'.
quatro — think 'quartet' (four players).
cinco — as in Cinco de Mayo, the fifth of May.
seis — from Latin sex (six), as in 'sextet'.
sete — Latin septem, as in 'September' (originally the seventh month).
oito — from Latin octo, as in 'octopus' (eight arms).
nove — Latin novem, as in 'November' (originally the ninth month).
dez — think 'decimal' and 'decade' (Latin decem, ten).
doze — same root as 'dozen' (twelve).
quinze — think 'quinceañera', the famous 15th-birthday party.
dezesseis — literally dez e seis, 'ten and six'.
dezessete — literally dez e sete, 'ten and seven'.
dezoito — dez + oito, 'ten-eight'.
dezenove — dez e nove, 'ten and nine'.
por favor — literally 'as a favor'.
talvez — literally tal + vez, 'such a turn/occasion' — perhaps.
só — from Latin solus; think 'solo': just, only, alone.
não — one nasal word covers both English 'no' and 'not'.
obrigado — literally 'obliged': I'm obligated to you.
você — contraction of vossa mercê, 'your mercy'.
vocês — você plus -s: 'you all'.
dela — literally de + ela, 'of her'.
nosso — from Latin noster, as in 'Cosa Nostra' ('our thing').
deles — literally de + eles, 'of them'.
qualquer — literally qual + quer, 'whichever (one) wants'.
só — think 'solo' (Latin solus): only, alone.
outro — from Latin alter ('alternate'); handily sounds like 'other'.
todo — same root as 'total' (Latin totus, whole).
por que — literally 'for what?'.
como — also means 'as/like' (and even 'I eat', from comer) — context decides.
qual — from Latin qualis; think 'quality' (of what kind).
amanhã — built on manhã ('morning'): 'on the morrow'.
manhã — Portuguese cousin of Spanish 'mañana'.
dia — think 'diary' (a day-book) or 'per diem' (by the day).
noite — same root as 'nocturnal' (Latin nox, night).
semana — from Latin septimana, 'a set of seven' days.
mês — from Latin mensis, as in 'semester' (six months).
ano — same root as 'annual' and 'anniversary'.
hora — near-twin of 'hour' (Latin hora).
minuto — near-twin of 'minute'.
segundo — near-twin of 'second'; also means second in order.
agora — from Latin hac hora, 'at this hour'.
antes — Latin ante, as in a.m. (ante meridiem, before noon).
depois — hides Latin post ('after'), as in 'postpone'.
mais tarde — literally 'more late'; tarde shares a root with 'tardy'.
próximo — same root as 'proximity': the next one is the nearest.
primavera — as in pasta primavera, named for its spring vegetables.
outono — same root as 'autumn' (Latin autumnus).
inverno — from Latin hibernus; think 'hibernate'.
fazer — from Latin facere; think 'factory', a place that makes things.
comer — think 'comestibles' (things to eat).
beber — same root as 'beverage' and 'imbibe'.
ler — from Latin legere; think 'legible' (readable).
ver — from Latin videre; think 'video' (literally 'I see').
começar — same root as 'commence'.
parar — same root as 'parry' and the para- in 'parachute' (it stops a fall).
dormir — same root as 'dormitory' and 'dormant'.
ter — from Latin tenere; think 'retain' and 'tenant' (one who holds).
vir — from Latin venire; think 'advent' (a coming) or 'venue'.
usar — near-twin of English 'use'.
pode — from poder ('can'); same root as 'potent' and 'power'.
